evals.prompt: We've never run a DR drill. We have a documented plan that says we can fail over to our secondary region in 15 minutes. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Start with a tabletop, not a live exercise. A tabletop walks through the documented plan in a guided discussion and surfaces obvious gaps before risking real infrastructure. Expect the first tabletop to find: outdated commands in the runbook, missing credentials, stale team references, untested assumptions about timing. Update the plan from those findings, then escalate to a partial live drill (in staging or with limited blast radius), then to a full production live drill only after the contained drills are clean. Do NOT start with a production live failover; a team's first live DR exercise should not be production-wide.
assertions: Recommends starting with a tabletop, not a live exercise
Lists the gaps a tabletop will surface (outdated commands, missing creds, stale references)
Recommends updating the plan after the tabletop
Recommends partial live in staging before full production
Warns against starting with a production live failover
Recommends the escalation: tabletop -> partial live -> full live
Treats the documented '15 minutes' as a hypothesis, not a fact

evals.prompt: We just ran a region-failover drill. It worked! The failover completed in 12 minutes, under our 15-minute target. Done?
evals.expected_output: Almost certainly not done. Every drill surfaces something; a drill that 'just worked' is likely a drill that wasn't real enough, or that the team didn't look hard enough at the gaps. Specific questions: did the engineers improvise any steps not in the runbook? Were there any manual interventions not documented? Did the pager fire to the on-call as expected, or were specific humans alerted out of band? Were any close calls or moments of 'we got lucky'? Was the data actually validated post-recovery, or just assumed? The drill report should include the unstated improvisations and close calls, not just the timing.
assertions: Treats 'it worked' as a likely incomplete drill, not a final result
Asks about unstated improvisations and manual steps
Asks whether the pager actually fired to the on-call
Asks about close calls and 'got lucky' moments
Asks about post-recovery data validation
References the 'no perfect drill' rule
Does not approve declaring done based on RTO alone

evals.prompt: We want to drill our database restore. The database is 4TB. Should we restore it in production, in staging, or just talk through it?
evals.expected_output: Restore the actual data in a separate environment, not production. The drill should verify the restore process works end-to-end, the restored data matches RPO, and the restore time matches RTO. Staging or a dedicated DR environment is the right place. Don't drill the restore in production unless you're restoring over a known-loss event; the restore is destructive if you misconfigure it. Don't do a tabletop alone for a database restore — the time and IO patterns of a 4TB restore can't be validated by discussion. The live exercise here is mandatory; staging is the right venue.
assertions: Recommends restoring in staging or a dedicated DR environment, not production
Warns against tabletop alone for a database restore drill
Identifies the live exercise as necessary to measure actual restore time and IO
Notes the restore is destructive if misconfigured in production
Validates RPO (data match) and RTO (time) as the success criteria
Does not recommend tabletop-only for a 4TB restore

evals.prompt: We've been running quarterly DR drills for two years. The last three drills each surfaced 5-10 action items. Almost none of the action items got done before the next drill. Each drill finds many of the same issues. What's going wrong?
evals.expected_output: The drill program is broken because the action items aren't getting closed. Drills are a forcing function for fixes; if the fixes don't happen, the drills become theater that surface the same issues every quarter. The fixes: every gap gets an action item with an owner and a deadline (not 'update the runbook' with no owner); action items go on the team's regular work tracker, not a one-off drill report; open action items block the next drill — don't run a new drill while previous action items are open; closed action items are verified by the next drill. Recommend pausing the drill cadence until the backlog is worked down, then restarting with strict action-item discipline.
assertions: Identifies action-item follow-through as the broken part
Recommends owners and deadlines for every action item
Recommends putting items on the regular work tracker, not a one-off doc
Recommends open action items block the next drill
Recommends pausing the drill cadence to work down the backlog
Notes drills become theater without action-item discipline
Recommends verifying closed items in the next drill

evals.prompt: Should we run our DR drill on a Friday afternoon so it doesn't disrupt mid-week productivity?
evals.expected_output: No. Friday afternoon is the worst time for any operation that could become an incident. The team's recovery capacity is lowest going into the weekend; if the drill exposes a real problem, the on-call inherits a weekend incident with reduced team availability. Run during a defined off-peak window when the team can stay engaged for the recovery if needed — typically a weekday morning. Customer notification matters too; coordinate with the broader org. Do not prioritize 'doesn't disrupt mid-week' over drill safety.
assertions: Recommends against Friday afternoon
Identifies the weekend recovery capacity issue if drill goes badly
Recommends a weekday off-peak window
Treats drill timing as a safety decision
Identifies customer notification as part of the coordination
Does not optimize for not disrupting other work over drill quality

evals.prompt: We do chaos engineering daily (random pod kills). Is that the same as having a DR program?
evals.expected_output: No. Chaos engineering and DR exercises are complementary but distinct. Chaos tests the system's resilience under small failures (a pod killed, a latency spike). DR exercises test the system's recovery from large-scale failures (region outage, primary DB loss, data corruption). A team doing only chaos may never test their real DR plan. The runbook for full region failover doesn't get exercised by Chaos Monkey killing one pod. Recommend running both: chaos for daily resilience, DR exercises quarterly for large-scale recovery. Chaos is often the precondition for DR (resilience to small failures makes large-failure recovery cleaner), but it doesn't replace DR.
assertions: Distinguishes chaos engineering from DR exercises
Identifies chaos as small-failure testing
Identifies DR exercises as large-failure recovery testing
Recommends running both
Notes that chaos doesn't test the DR runbook
Identifies chaos as often a precondition for graceful DR
Does not equate chaos engineering with DR program

evals.prompt: Our identity provider (Okta) went down last month and we couldn't log into any of our internal tools to respond to the incident. Should we drill that scenario?
evals.expected_output: Yes — and prioritize it. An identity-provider-down scenario is high-blast-radius because it disables the normal access path the on-call uses to respond to other incidents. Drill the breakglass auth path: verify the on-call can actually log in via the emergency procedure, that the procedure is documented and current, that the credentials exist and work. This is also a natural drill to combine with audit: when was the breakglass last tested? Who has access? Are the credentials rotated after use? Recommend a tabletop first to surface the documented plan, then a partial live drill where the on-call actually invokes breakglass.
assertions: Recommends drilling the scenario
Identifies identity-provider-down as high-blast-radius (disables normal access)
Recommends drilling the breakglass auth path
Verifies the breakglass procedure is current and credentials work
Connects to broader breakglass hygiene (rotation after use, who has access)
Recommends tabletop first, then partial live drill
References the recent real incident as motivating the drill
